title: Fitting a sliding bolt on a shed or outbuilding door
subtitle: Fix a surface-mounted barrel bolt to secure a shed, workshop, or outbuilding door from the outside or inside
excerpt: How to fit a surface-mounted sliding barrel bolt to a shed, gate, or outbuilding door. Covers choosing the bolt size, positioning, drilling pilot holes, and aligning the keep plate for a smooth, positive action.
sourceNotes: UK building trade practice for outbuilding security hardware. Reader's Diges Complete DIY Manual (referenced, not reproduced).

glossaryTerms.definition: A surface-mounted sliding fastener in which a cylindrical steel bar slides in a guide to engage a keep plate fixed to the door frame or opposite leaf. The bolt is operated by hand (no key required) and latches by friction or a positive detent click. Available in sizes from 50 mm (cabinet door) to 300 mm (gate or heavy door). Galvanised steel bolts are suited to outdoor and damp conditions; brass or chrome bolts are for interior use.

content.text: secures a shed or outbuilding door with a simple hand-operated sliding bar. The approach is fixing the bolt body to the door, marking the keep position from the extended bolt, and fixing the keep to the door frame or post. The job does not need a pilot drill, but drilling pilots prevents splitting in dry softwood.

For a shed or workshop door, use a galvanised or zinc-plated bolt at least 150 mm long (a longer bolt is harder to force. For an internal door or gate leaf, 100 mm is usually sufficient. Buy a bolt rated for outdoor use if the fitting is exposed to weather; a cheap steel bolt will rust solid within one season.

Hold the bolt body on the door face at the chosen position) typically 150 to 200 mm from the top or bottom of the door. The bolt should clear any door furniture already fitted. Mark the screw holes with a pencil through the bolt fixing flanges. Drill pilot holes at 2 mm diameter for the provided screws. Fix the bolt body to the door face.

Close the door fully. Extend the bolt to its full travel. The tip of the bolt marks the centre of the keep position on the door frame or post. Hold the keep plate over this mark and check that the bolt tip centres on the keep opening. Mark and drill the keep screw holes. Fix the keep. Test the bolt: it should slide smoothly and engage the keep without binding or requiring force. If the bolt tip catches on the keep edge, shift the keep up or down by 1 to 2 mm.

attrs.symptom: The bolt is stiff to slide after a few months outdoors
attrs.cause: Rust or paint galling the bolt barrel inside the guide.
attrs.fix: Apply a dry lubricant or petroleum jelly to the bolt barrel. If heavily rusted, remove the bolt, clean with emery cloth, and apply a thin coat of grease before refitting. A galvanised bolt prevents this.
